policy_statement: TMG will support remote disaster response and business continuity by identifying operations that can be handled remotely, supporting SMBs in BCP formulation and telework introduction, and verifying the Central Market BCP while supporting BCPs for market-related businesses.
mechanism_description: The city plans to identify functions that can be handled remotely and support businesses in business continuity planning and telework so operations can continue during disasters.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard event → continuity planning + remote operations → ↑ institutional adaptive capacity → ↓ disruption/vulnerability
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes: City agencies and businesses identify remotely executable functions, establish telework capability (IT systems, protocols), and create/test business continuity plans. (2) Mediating process: During disasters (e.g., storms, floods, heat emergencies), remote work and pre-planned continuity procedures reduce dependence on disrupted transport/physical workplaces and improve coordination and response. (3) Outcome: Reduced service outages and economic disruption, improved response and recovery, and lower vulnerability of institutions and communities to climate shocks.
dominant_pathway_test: Enabling telework and continuity plans most strongly reduces climate-disaster impacts by keeping critical functions operating and reducing disruption during hazard events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazard → continuity measures/telework → increased adaptive capacity → reduced disruption and vulnerability.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: The core purpose is to maintain operations during disasters (resilience/continuity), not to reduce energy use (Resource Efficiency) or directly cut GHGs (Mitigation). Any emissions effects from telework (reduced commuting) are incidental and not the dominant climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific telework policy is explicitly framed/quantified primarily as commuting-emissions reduction, it could shift toward Mitigation/Resource Efficiency at the policy level; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly links telework to disaster continuity and adaptive capacity.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: “Business continuity planning + telework enablement for disasters” consistently functions as an adaptation capacity-building measure across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence (e.g., whether focused on critical infrastructure continuity, economic resilience, emergency management, cybersecurity/communications).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Telework can also reduce transport emissions (a mitigation co-benefit), but under this mechanism the dominant intent/effect is continuity during climate/disaster disruptions (adaptation). If implemented mainly for day-to-day congestion/energy savings rather than hazard continuity, classification could differ at the policy level.
key_indicators: "Ensuring business continuity"; "Identify operations for which disaster response can be made remotely"; "Support small- and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) in formulating BCPs"; "promoting the introduction of telework"; "continuously verifying the Disaster Preparedness Manual (Central Market BCP)"; "supporting the formulation of BCPs"
co_benefits: Improved general disaster preparedness beyond climate-specific hazards; productivity/flexibility gains from telework; potential reductions in commuting-related emissions/air pollution (not explicit).
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes elements that could be coded as an incentive/technical assistance program for SMBs (support for BCPs/telework), but no specific funding mechanism is stated; overall instrument is best captured as a continuity-planning strategy with support activities.
